hints: Known as 'King Kohli', he holds the record for the most centuries in ODI history, surpassing his idol's tally of 49.
The first player to score 50 centuries in the ODI format, achieving the feat during the 2023 World Cup semi-final.
Averaged over 50 in all three international formats simultaneously for a significant period of his career.
The only player in cricket history to score three double centuries in One Day Internationals.
Holds the record for the highest individual score in an ODI, smashing 264 against Sri Lanka in 2014.
Hit a record-breaking five centuries in a single edition of the ICC Cricket World Cup (2019).
Famous for his unique hyper-extended bowling action and being a master of the toe-crushing yorker.
The fastest Indian pacer to reach 150 wickets in Test matches, though he remains equally lethal in the white-ball death overs.
Played a pivotal role in India's 2024 T20 World Cup win, earning the Player of the Tournament award.
The first male cricketer to score a double century in ODIs, achieving it against South Africa in 2010.
He finished his career with exactly 100 international centuries and is the all-time leading run-scorer in both Tests and ODIs.
Participated in six different World Cups (1992–2011), finally winning the trophy on his home ground in Mumbai.
Named the Man of the Match in the 1999 World Cup final after taking 4 for 33 to dismantle Pakistan.
The legendary leg-spinner who revived the art of wrist-spin, famously bowling the 'Ball of the Century' to Mike Gatting.
Captained the Rajasthan Royals to victory in the inaugural edition of the Indian Premier League in 2008.
Captained India to its maiden World Cup victory in 1983, famously taking a running catch to dismiss Viv Richards in the final.
Smashed an unbeaten 175 against Zimbabwe in 1983 after India was reeling at 17/5, an innings that was never televised due to a strike.
Never missed a single match in his career due to injury, a remarkable feat for a premier fast-bowling all-rounder.
The 'Universe Boss' is the first player to hit a double century in a World Cup match (215 vs Zimbabwe, 2015).
Holds the record for the most sixes in international cricket and the highest individual score in T20 history (175*).
Known for his laid-back style, he is one of only four players to score two triple centuries in Test matches.
The only bowler to take three hat-tricks in ODI cricket and the first to take four wickets in four consecutive balls twice.
Famous for his 'slingshot' action and blonde-streaked curls, he was the primary architect of Sri Lanka's 2014 T20 World Cup win.
Is the leading wicket-taker for the Mumbai Indians in the IPL, where his yorkers became legendary.
The most successful captain in international cricket history, leading Australia to two consecutive World Cup titles in 2003 and 2007.
Smashed a blistering 140* in the 2003 World Cup final against India, effectively ending the contest in the first innings.
Known as 'Punter', he is Australia's all-time leading run-scorer in both Test and ODI cricket.
Known as the 'Sultan of Swing', he was the first bowler to reach 500 wickets in ODI cricket.
The Man of the Match in the 1992 World Cup final, where his back-to-back deliveries to Allan Lamb and Chris Lewis turned the game.
Famous for his mastery of reverse swing, he took two Test hat-tricks and two ODI hat-tricks during his career.
Became the second batsman in history to score an ODI double century, smashing 219 against the West Indies in 2011.
Known for his 'see ball, hit ball' philosophy, he famously hit a boundary off the first ball of the match in five different games during the 2011 World Cup.
One of only four batsmen to score two triple centuries in Test matches, though his ODI strike rate remains one of the highest of his era.
Famously used a squash ball inside his glove to score a match-winning 149 in the 2007 World Cup final against Sri Lanka.
Revolutionized the role of a wicketkeeper-batsman, maintaining a career ODI strike rate of nearly 97 as an aggressive opener.
The first player to hit 100 sixes in Test cricket, he was a key pillar in Australia's three consecutive World Cup triumphs (1999, 2003, 2007).
Named the Player of the Tournament in the 2011 World Cup for his incredible all-round contribution of 362 runs and 15 wickets.
Famously hit six sixes in an over off Stuart Broad during the inaugural T20 World Cup in 2007.
Heroically played through the 2011 World Cup while battling a germ cell tumor, later making a successful return to international cricket after recovery.
The only captain in history to win all three major ICC trophies: the T20 World Cup (2007), the ODI World Cup (2011), and the Champions Trophy (2013).
Finished the 2011 World Cup final with a legendary six over long-on, ending India's 28-year wait for the trophy.
Widely regarded as the greatest finisher in ODI history, he finished his career with an average over 50 despite batting largely in the middle order.
Nicknamed the 'Rawalpindi Express', he was the first bowler to officially break the 100 mph (161 km/h) barrier in international cricket.
Achieved his record speed during a 2003 World Cup match against England, bowling a delivery to Nick Knight.
Known for his incredibly long run-up and explosive pace, he remained one of the most feared fast bombers despite a career hampered by injuries.
The 'Scientist' of the cricket world who treats every delivery as a new experiment, using carrom balls and subtle variations to outsmart opponents.
A vital cog in the 2011 World Cup-winning campaign, he has consistently evolved his game to remain relevant across three decades of white-ball cricket.
A very handy lower-order batter with multiple Test centuries, he brings a high-level tactical brain to every aspect of the game.
Winner of the Golden Ball in the 2013 Champions Trophy, taking 12 wickets to help India lift the trophy.
Known as 'Sir Jadeja', he is one of the most prolific fielders in the world and the first Indian left-arm spinner to take 200 ODI wickets.
Smashed a nearly match-winning 77 off 59 balls in the 2019 World Cup semi-final, showcasing his evolution into a top-tier all-rounder.
Revolutionized ODI batting by utilizing the first 15 overs for aggressive pinch-hitting during the 1996 World Cup.
The only player in ODI history to achieve the double of 10,000 runs and 300 wickets.
Nicknamed the 'Matara Mauler', he once held the record for the fastest ODI century and the most sixes in an innings.
The leading run-scorer of the 1999 World Cup, where he successfully transitioned from a Test specialist to an ODI mainstay.
Known as 'The Wall', he famously kept wickets for India during the 2003 World Cup to help the team balance its combination.
Involved in two of the largest partnerships in ODI history, both exceeding 300 runs, alongside Sourav Ganguly and Sachin Tendulkar.
Famously scored a painfully slow 36* off 174 balls against England in the first-ever World Cup match in 1975.
Redeemed his ODI record by scoring his only century in the format—a blistering 103* off 88 balls—against New Zealand in the 1987 World Cup.
The first player to reach 10,000 runs in Test cricket, he was a member of India's 1983 World Cup-winning squad.
Holds the record for the highest individual score in Test cricket (400*) and first-class cricket (501*).
Scored a magnificent 111 in the 1996 World Cup quarter-final against South Africa to lead the West Indies into the semis.
Known for his high backlift and flourishing style, he remains the only West Indian to surpass 10,000 runs in both Tests and ODIs.
Nicknamed 'The Big Show', he played arguably the greatest ODI innings of all time, scoring 201* while battling severe cramps to win a match against Afghanistan from 91/7 in the 2023 World Cup.
Holds the record for the fastest century in Men's World Cup history, reaching the milestone in just 40 balls against the Netherlands in 2023.
A dynamic all-rounder known for his array of '360-degree' shots, particularly the reverse sweep and switch hit.
Held the record for the highest individual score in ODI history for 13 years after smashing 194 against India in 1997.
Widely regarded as Pakistan's greatest-ever ODI opener, known for his elegant wrist-work and placement through the off-side.
Scored three consecutive ODI centuries in Sharjah in 1993, becoming only the second player in history to achieve the feat at the time.
Became the fastest player to reach 5,000 ODI runs, achieving the milestone in just 97 innings to beat Hashim Amla's record.
The first Pakistani captain to lead his side to the world number 1 ranking in the ICC ODI Team Rankings in 2023.
Known for his textbook technique and his signature cover drive, he held the number 1 spot in the ICC ODI batting rankings for over 900 days.
The fastest bowler to reach 100 ODI wickets, achieving the feat in just 44 matches.
Named the ICC Men's T20I Player of the Decade, though he is equally dominant in ODIs with a career bowling average under 20.
Became the youngest player to captain an international side at the age of 19 during the 2018 World Cup Qualifiers.
The charismatic captain who led Pakistan to its only ODI World Cup title in 1992, famously urging his team to 'fight like cornered tigers'.
One of the legendary 'quartet' of all-rounders in the 1980s alongside Ian Botham, Kapil Dev, and Richard Hadlee.
Finished his career on the ultimate high by taking the final wicket to win the 1992 World Cup final against England.
The 'Original Master Blaster' who walked to the crease with a swagger and a piece of gum that suggested the bowlers should be apologizing to him.
He never wore a helmet, even against the most terrifying pace, and dominated the 1970s and 80s with a strike rate that was decades ahead of its time.
His 189* against England is still considered one of the greatest ODI innings ever, played with a level of arrogance and genius that defined an era.
Top-scored for India in the finals of both the 2007 T20 World Cup (75) and the 2011 ODI World Cup (97).
Known for his grit and big-match temperament, he shared a crucial 109-run partnership with MS Dhoni to stabilize the 2011 World Cup final.
The only Indian to score centuries in five consecutive Test matches, though he was equally a pillar for the ODI top order for nearly a decade.
The 'Captain Cool' of Australia who led his side to the 2023 World Cup title, famously silencing a crowd of 100,000 in the final.
A pacer with an unrelenting motor and a relentless accuracy that makes him the ultimate 'big-game' player for the Baggy Green.
Possesses a very handy lower-order batting ability, often providing the crucial runs needed to secure a victory in tight run chases.
The first player to score centuries in each of his first three Test matches, later becoming a prolific run-scorer and captain in ODIs.
Famous for his supple wrists and artistic strokeplay, he led India in three consecutive World Cups (1992, 1996, and 1999).
Held the record for the most runs and most matches in ODI history at the time of his retirement.
India's leading wicket-taker in ODI history with 337 scalps, known for his unique style of leg-spin that relied on pace and bounce.
Held the record for the best bowling figures by an Indian in ODIs (6/12 vs West Indies, 1993) for nearly 21 years.
One of only two bowlers to take all 10 wickets in a Test innings, he was also the leading wicket-taker in the 1996 World Cup.
The first Indian pacer to take 300 ODI wickets, he spearheaded the attack throughout the 1990s and early 2000s.
Jointly holds the record for the most wickets by an Indian in World Cup history (44), level with Zaheer Khan.
Nicknamed the 'Mysore Express', he was one of the fastest bowlers India produced, consistently clocking over 145 kph in his prime.
The first Indian player to score centuries in all three formats of international cricket.
A vital middle-order engine room for India, he played two high-pressure cameos against Australia and Pakistan to help win the 2011 World Cup.
Known for his exceptional fielding and 'team-man' spirit, he was the first Indian to reach 5,000 runs in the IPL.
Nicknamed 'Gabbar', he famously scored a century on his Test debut in just 85 balls, the fastest by any debutant.
A master of ICC tournaments, he won the Golden Bat in the 2013 Champions Trophy and was India's leading run-scorer in the 2015 World Cup.
Known for his trademark 'thigh-five' celebration after taking a catch, he formed one of India's most successful opening pairs with Rohit Sharma.
The 'Mustache' that haunted India in 2023, scoring a match-winning century in the World Cup final to lead Australia to their sixth title.
A fearless left-handed opener who treats the powerplay as a personal target range, often deciding the fate of a match in the first ten overs.
Known for his aggressive intent and a safe pair of hands in the field, he is also a very capable off-spinner who specializes in breaking stubborn partnerships.
The first Pakistani to win the Sir Garfield Sobers Trophy for ICC Men's Cricketer of the Year (2021).
Famous for his lethal first-over spells, particularly his ability to bowl fast, swinging yorkers to dismiss top-order right-handers.
At age 19, he recorded the best bowling figures by a Pakistani in World Cup history, taking 6 for 35 against Bangladesh in 2019.
The most metronomic fast bowler in history who famously predicted a 5-0 series win every time he woke up in the morning.
He didn't need 150 kph pace when he could hit the same postage stamp on the pitch for fifteen years straight until the batsman gave up.
His batting was so notoriously bad that his career-high of 61 in Tests is still considered one of the great miracles of the modern age.
The ultimate World Cup specialist who owns the record for the most wickets in a single tournament and treats the opening over like a personal audition for a highlight reel.
A left-arm thunderbolt merchant whose inswinging yorker is widely considered the most unplayable delivery in modern white-ball cricket.
Possesses a golden run in ICC events, leading the Australian attack with a level of hostility and pace that turns games in the space of three balls.
Known as the original 'Mystery Spinner', he introduced the 'carrom ball' to the international stage, flicking the ball with his middle finger.
Destroyed India in the 2008 Asia Cup final, taking 6 for 13 to announce himself as a global sensation.
Holds the record for the fastest bowler to reach 50 ODI wickets, achieving the feat in just 19 matches.
The first Indian bowler to take two international hat-tricks, achieving them against Australia (2017) and the West Indies (2019).
A rare left-arm wrist-spinner (Chinaman), he became the fastest Indian spinner to reach 150 ODI wickets.
Played a pivotal role in India's 2023 Asia Cup win, including a memorable 5-wicket haul against Pakistan.
Finished as the leading wicket-taker of the 2023 World Cup (24 wickets) despite not playing the first four matches of the tournament.
Became the first Indian to take a 7-wicket haul in an ODI, recording figures of 7 for 57 in the 2023 World Cup semi-final against New Zealand.
The second Indian to take a World Cup hat-trick, achieving the feat against Afghanistan in 2019.
The only bowler in history to take a hat-trick in the first over of a Test match, achieved against Pakistan at Karachi in 2006.
A vital all-rounder who was named Man of the Match in the 2007 T20 World Cup final for his spell of 3 for 16.
Often compared to Wasim Akram early in his career due to his natural ability to swing the ball into the right-hander at high pace.
A versatile technician who can open the innings with classical poise or finish games as a middle-order wicketkeeper-batsman.
He maintains an extraordinary average of over 50, often acting as the tactical bridge that stabilizes the Indian innings when the top order fails.
Known for his 'shut out the noise' celebration, he provides a level of technical elegance and game-reading ability that makes him indispensable in high-pressure chases.
A 'tape-ball' sensation from Rawalpindi who rose through the ranks to become one of the fastest and most aggressive pacers in the world.
Possesses a deceptive bouncer and a 150 kph thunderbolt that makes him a terrifying prospect for batters during the middle and death overs.
He reached 100 ODI wickets in record time for a Pakistani pacer, relying on raw intensity and a competitive spirit that thrives under pressure.
Captained Australia to their fifth World Cup title in 2015, scoring a half-century in the final before retiring from the format.
Nicknamed 'Pup', he made a sensational Test debut with 151 against India in Bangalore in 2004.
Once took 6 wickets for just 9 runs in a Test innings against India in Mumbai, showcasing his underrated left-arm orthodox spin.
Nicknamed 'The Turbanator', he was the first Indian to take a Test hat-trick, achieved during the legendary 2001 Border-Gavaskar series.
A key member of both of India's World Cup-winning squads (2007 T20 and 2011 ODI).
He finished his career as one of India's most successful off-spinners, famously sharing a long-standing rivalry with Ricky Ponting.
The joint-leading wicket-taker of the 2011 World Cup (21 wickets), widely credited as the architect of India's bowling success in that tournament.
A master of the knuckleball and reverse swing, he is regarded as one of India's greatest-ever left-arm fast bowlers.
Famous for his spell at Trent Bridge in 2007, where he took 9 wickets in the match to lead India to a rare series win in England.
The first player to score a century in a World Cup final (2011) and end up on the losing side.
Known for his silky touch and masterclass captaincy, he led Sri Lanka to the 2007 World Cup final and the 2014 T20 World Cup title.
Shared a world-record partnership of 624 runs with Kumar Sangakkara in a Test match against South Africa in 2006.
Became the first player to score four consecutive centuries in a single World Cup tournament, achieved during the 2015 edition.
A prolific run-scorer and refined wicketkeeper who finished as the second-highest run-getter in ODI history behind Sachin Tendulkar.
Famously delivered a powerful and political MCC Spirit of Cricket Cowdrey Lecture in 2011 while still an active player.
The first player to take a hat-trick in T20 International history, though he was primarily a dominant force in ODIs with 380 wickets.
Clocked at a top speed of 161.1 km/h (100.1 mph) in 2005, he was the primary pace spearhead for Australia after the McGrath era.
A key member of the 2003 World Cup-winning squad, he took a hat-trick against Kenya during the tournament's Super Six stage.
A wrist-spinner who bowled off-breaks with a 'doosra' that was so confusing it led to a decade of biomechanical debates and laboratory tests.
Possessed wide, bulging eyes and a smile that suggested he was having the time of his life while systematically destroying your middle order.
The leading wicket-taker in both formats, he turned every dry pitch in the world into a minefield where the ball turned a different way every time.
The ultimate cricketing machine who could walk into any all-time XI solely as a batter or solely as a fast bowler.
Often criticized for his strike rate, but he was the immovable insurance policy that allowed the rest of the South African lineup to explode.
His statistical record is so immense that he basically spent fifteen years being two world-class players in one body.
Renowned for his lethal 'banana' inswinging yorkers, he holds the record for the most five-wicket hauls in ODI history (13).
Formed one half of the 'Two Ws' partnership alongside Wasim Akram, terrorizing batsmen with reverse swing in the 90s.
Became the youngest-ever captain of Pakistan in Test cricket at age 22, later leading the side in the 2003 World Cup.
Statistically the greatest all-rounder in the history of Bangladesh cricket, consistently ranking as the ICC No. 1 in all formats.
Produced a historic performance in the 2019 World Cup, becoming the first player to score 600+ runs and take 10+ wickets in a single edition.
One of only a few players in history to have achieved the double of 7,000 runs and 300 wickets in One Day Internationals.
The first Australian cricketer in 132 years to be selected for a national team in any format without playing a single match of first-class cricket.
Scored seven ODI centuries in the year 2016 alone, equalling the record for the second-most hundreds in a calendar year.
A two-time World Cup winner (2015, 2023) known for his aggressive opening style and exceptional agility in the field.
A national hero who rose to fame as a 22-year-old by scoring a match-winning 60 off 37 balls in the 1992 World Cup semi-final.
Despite his reputation for being involved in comical run-outs, he is Pakistan's all-time leading run-scorer in ODI cricket.
Known for his immense strength and 'lazy elegance,' he was particularly dominant against express fast bowling throughout the 90s.
The 'Steyn Remover' who possessed a bowling action so smooth it was practically poetic, until the ball reached 150 kph and started swinging late.
A pacer with a bulging neck vein and a celebration that looked like a warrior's roar, he was the most terrifying sight in cricket for nearly a decade.
He took nearly 200 ODI wickets by combining raw, unadulterated pace with an outswinger that world-class openers still see in their nightmares.
Nicknamed 'Boom Boom', he famously scored a 37-ball century in his first international innings, a record that stood for over 17 years.
Originally a pinch-hitting opener, he evolved into a premier leg-spinner, finishing as one of the highest wicket-takers in ODI history.
Led Pakistan to the semi-finals of the 2011 World Cup, finishing the tournament as the joint-leading wicket-taker with 21 scalps.
Known as 'Sixer Sidhu' for his ability to repeatedly charge and loft spinners for massive sixes during the 1987 and 1996 World Cups.
Successfully transitioned from a defensive 'strokeless wonder' to an aggressive white-ball opener after being criticized by the media.
A key figure in India's 1996 World Cup campaign, scoring a vital 93 against Pakistan in the high-voltage quarter-final at Bengaluru.
The smiling assassin of the 90s Indian middle order, famous for his high-energy running and for dismantling Waqar Younis in the 1996 World Cup quarter-final.
A brilliant fielder and a clever finisher whose career ended abruptly due to a match-fixing ban just as he was at his peak.
He was the prototype for the modern 'bits and pieces' player who could contribute in all three departments while maintaining a permanent grin.
Immortalized for hitting a six off the final ball of the match against Chetan Sharma to win the 1986 Austral-Asia Cup in Sharjah.
The first player to appear in six different World Cup tournaments, a record later equalled only by Sachin Tendulkar.
Known for his tactical brilliance and 'street-smart' batting, he was the backbone of the Pakistan middle order for two decades.
Famously clean-bowled Aamir Sohail in the 1996 World Cup quarter-final immediately after being gestured at by the batsman.
Formed a successful new-ball partnership with Javagal Srinath, known for his subtle leg-cutters and deceptive slower balls.
Took 5 for 27 against Pakistan in the 1999 World Cup, which remains one of the best bowling figures by an Indian in the tournament.
The first bowler to dismiss Sachin Tendulkar for a duck in Indian domestic first-class cricket.
Known for his exquisite control over swing, he became the first Indian to take a five-wicket haul in all three international formats.
Shared a match-winning 100-run partnership with MS Dhoni for the 8th wicket against Sri Lanka in 2017, scoring an unbeaten 53.
A versatile top-order batsman who played a crucial role in India's 2015 World Cup campaign, including a standout 79 against South Africa.
Known for his impeccable technique and calmness, he famously led India to a historic Test series win in Australia in 2020-21.
Holds the world record for the most catches by a non-wicketkeeper in a single Test match (8).
A 6-foot-4-inch giant who arrived as a teenager with raw pace and famously set the 2013 Champions Trophy final alight with a game-changing double-wicket over.
Before becoming a Test specialist, he was a regular in the Indian ODI side, using his height to extract uncomfortable bounce and seam movement.
He is one of the most durable pacers in Indian history, reinventing his game over a 15-year career to remain a potent force on any surface.
Became the first middle-order batsman for India to score over 500 runs in a single edition of the ODI World Cup (2023).
Known for his aggressive intent against spin, he smashed two consecutive centuries during the 2023 World Cup campaign.
Captained the Delhi Capitals to their first-ever IPL final in 2020 before later leading the Kolkata Knight Riders to the title.
India's leading wicket-taker in the 2015 World Cup, finishing with 18 scalps at an average of 17.83.
One of the fastest bowlers India has produced, he was the first pacer from the Vidarbha region to play for the national side.
Known for his natural outswing and high pace, he has taken over 100 wickets in both Test and ODI cricket.
The youngest player in history to score an ODI double century, smashing 208 against New Zealand at the age of 23.
Became the fastest player to reach 2,000 ODI runs, achieving the milestone in just 38 innings.
Won the Orange Cap in the 2023 IPL for scoring 890 runs, the second-highest tally in a single season in tournament history.
Captained Australia to World Cup glory in 1999, famously scoring a century against South Africa after being 'dropped' by Herschelle Gibbs.
One of the primary pioneers of the 'slower ball back-of-the-hand' delivery in the late 80s and early 90s.
Known for his mental toughness, he oversaw Australia's record run of 16 consecutive Test victories.
An incredibly long-serving all-rounder whose international career spanned four different decades (1990s to 2020s).
One of the few players to have scored over 7,000 runs and taken 150 wickets in One Day Internationals.
Captained Pakistan to the final of the inaugural T20 World Cup in 2007.
The powerhouse all-rounder who overcame a career of hamstring issues to become an indispensable opening batter and a very effective seam bowler.
Famously smashed two centuries in the 2009 Champions Trophy knockout stages and once hit 185* against Bangladesh with 15 massive sixes.
Possessed a front-foot plant so iconic that it became a magnet for LBW decisions, yet he remains one of Australia's most decorated limited-overs stars.
Holds the record for the fastest ODI half-century by an Indian, reaching 50 in just 21 balls against Zimbabwe.
For a significant period, he held the world record for being the fastest bowler to reach 50 ODI wickets (23 matches).
Famously scored a Test century at Lord's in 2002, a feat that eluded many legendary Indian batsmen.
The first and only Pakistani batsman to score 10,000 runs in Test cricket.
Captained Pakistan to victory in the 2009 T20 World Cup held in England.
Known for his gritty middle-order batting and exceptional catching at slip, he scored over 7,000 runs in the ODI format.
Holds the world record for the most ODI career runs (5,122) without scoring a single century.
Captained Pakistan to the No. 1 Test ranking for the first time in 2016.
Famously led the side during the 'spot-fixing' recovery era, earning the nickname 'Man of Steel' for his resilience.
The bespectacled boy-wonder who turned into New Zealand's greatest spin bowler, captain, and occasionally their most reliable middle-order batter.
A master of drift and deception who survived on a diet of flight and accuracy while everyone else was trying to bowl 150 kph.
He looked like a quiet librarian but captained with a ruthless tactical mind, often carrying the entire Kiwi bowling attack on his back for years.
The 'Mighty Hash' who broke almost every record for being the fastest to reach 2,000, 3,000, 4,000, 5,000, 6,000, and 7,000 ODI runs.
Possessed wrists of silk and a serene temperament, accumulating centuries with the frequency of a man checking items off a grocery list.
He was the elegant counterpoint to South Africa's power-hitters, providing a level of consistency that stabilized the top order for over a decade.
A versatile all-rounder who famously opened both the batting and the bowling for India in the same match on 45 occasions.
Known for his ability to bowl deceptive slower balls and being a stubborn lower-order batsman, he was a fixture in the 1987 and 1992 World Cup squads.
His career ended controversy following the 1996 World Cup after he was taken apart by Sanath Jayasuriya in a group stage match.
New Zealand's all-time leading run-scorer in ODI cricket, known for his signature slog-sweep over mid-wicket.
Smashed a career-best 181* against England in 2018 while playing on one leg due to a severe quad injury.
The first cricketer to play 100 international matches in all three formats of the game.
The surgical anchor of England's 'new-age' white-ball team, accumulating runs with a strike rate that looks slow but is secretly devastating.
He was the highest run-scorer for England in their 2019 World Cup-winning campaign, providing the stability that allowed the power-hitters to go berserk.
Possesses a baby-faced grin and a technique so sound it makes him nearly impossible to tie down during the middle overs of an innings.
A former competitive chess player who represented India at the youth level before turning to professional cricket.
The first Indian bowler to take a 6-wicket haul in T20Is, he also took a memorable 6 for 42 in an ODI against Australia at Melbourne.
Known for his tactical intelligence and brave leg-spin, he formed a potent 'Kul-Cha' partnership with Kuldeep Yadav.
Started his career as a leg-spinner batting at number eight before evolving into one of the most obsessive and prolific run-scorers in history.
Possesses a fidgety, idiosyncratic technique that involves constant shuffling, yet he finds the gap with the surgical precision of a grandmaster.
A vital part of Australia's 2015 and 2023 World Cup triumphs, providing the middle-order glue that allows the power-hitters to play freely.
A baby-faced left-hander who plays with the casual aggression of a man playing a Sunday afternoon game in his backyard.
The only South African to score three consecutive centuries in an ODI series against India, a feat of pure dominance at a very young age.
A wicketkeeper-opener who treats the powerplay like a personal target range, clearing the infield with effortless flick shots and pulls.
Australia's T20 World Cup-winning captain and a powerhouse opener who once held the record for the highest individual T20I score.
A dominant force in ODI cricket for nearly a decade, he scored 17 centuries and formed one of the most feared opening pairs of the modern era with David Warner.
Despite his aggressive reputation, he was a shrewd tactical leader who took over the captaincy during one of the most difficult periods in Australian cricket history.
Hero of the 2002 NatWest Series final, scoring an unbeaten 87 to lead India to a historic chase at Lord's.
Regarded as one of India's greatest-ever fielders, he revolutionized the team's standards alongside Yuvraj Singh.
Captained the Indian U-19 team to their first-ever World Cup title in 2000.
Holds the record for the highest individual score in World Cup history, an unbeaten 237 against the West Indies in 2015.
Famous for being a 'two-toed' cricketer after a forklift accident in his youth, which didn't stop him from becoming an elite athlete.
Delivered the iconic run-out of MS Dhoni in the 2019 World Cup semi-final, a moment that shifted the course of the match.
The most polite assassin in world cricket, who will dismantle your entire bowling plan and then apologize for the inconvenience.
The tactical genius who led New Zealand to consecutive World Cup finals with a level of grace and sportsmanship that won over the planet.
He bats with a surgical precision, specializing in the late dab and the perfect placement that makes a 300-run chase look like a walk in the park.
The first Bangladeshi batsman to score a double century in Test cricket, though he is equally a pillar of their ODI middle order.
Known as 'Mr. Dependable', he has participated in five different ODI World Cups for Bangladesh.
A diminutive but gritty wicketkeeper-batsman who played a starring role in Bangladesh's 2015 World Cup win over England.
Nicknamed 'Mr. 360' for his ability to hit the ball to all parts of the ground with unconventional shots.
Holds the record for the fastest ODI century, reaching the milestone in just 31 balls against the West Indies in 2015.
Finished his career with an extraordinary combination of an average over 50 and a strike rate over 100 in ODI cricket.
The captain who led England to their first T20 World Cup title in 2022 and was a key figure in the 2019 ODI World Cup win.
Holds the three fastest ODI centuries for England, including one off just 46 balls against Pakistan.
A pioneer of the 'scoop' and 'ramp' shots, he is widely regarded as one of the most innovative white-ball batters in history.
A dynamic all-rounder known for his dreadlocks, zinc cream, and immense power, he was a key part of Australia's 2003 and 2007 World Cup wins.
